full_response:
We want the velocity of the rocket as measured in the primed inertial frame R′.

Given  
• Velocity of R′ with respect to R (along +x): v = v′ = 3c/5 = 0.60 c  
• Velocity of the rocket with respect to R (along +x): u = 4c/5 = 0.80 c  

Because all motion is colinear (x–direction), we need only the x–component of the special-relativistic velocity–addition law.  
From the formula sheet

 u = (u′ + v) / (1 + u′v/c²)    (1)

Solve (1) for u′ (the rocket’s velocity in R′):

 u′ = (u – v) / (1 – uv/c²).    (2)

Insert the given numbers:

Numerator: u – v = 0.80 c – 0.60 c = 0.20 c  
Denominator: 1 – uv/c² = 1 – (0.80)(0.60) = 1 – 0.48 = 0.52

Therefore

 u′ = (0.20 c)/(0.52) = (0.20/0.52) c = (5/13) c ≈ 0.385 c.

The positive sign means the rocket still moves in the +x direction in frame R′, but more slowly than in R.

\boxed{u' = \dfrac{5}{13}\,c \;\;(\text{along } +x)}  

Confidence: 86%
